perturbation.original_text: XIV. Remedies . The Receiving Party understands and acknowledges that any disclosure or misappropriation of any of the Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement may cause the Disclosing Party irreparable harm, the amount of which may be difficult to ascertain and, therefore, agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for an order restraining any such further disclosure or misappropriation and for such other relief as the Disclosing Party shall deem appropriate. Such right of the Disclosing Party shall be in addition to Remedies otherwise available to the Disclosing Party at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: XIV. Remedies . The Receiving Party understands and acknowledges that any disclosure or misappropriation of any of the Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement may cause the Disclosing Party irreparable harm, the amount of which may be difficult to ascertain. Such right of the Disclosing Party shall be in addition to Remedies otherwise available to the Disclosing Party at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific language allowing the Disclosing Party to seek injunctive relief from a court, the remedies section now implies that only monetary damages are available, which might not be sufficient to address the irreparable harm typically associated with a breach of confidentiality. Injunctive relief is a crucial remedy in NDA enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 38 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 allows for preventive relief by way of injunction. The modified clause removes the specific mention of court intervention, which will give rise to the argument that the parties have, by agreement, opted for a remedy that does not include injunctive relief, thereby contradicting the spirit and intent of Section 38, which aims to prevent breaches of contract, including those pertaining to confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifically allows the Disclosing Party to seek injunctive relief from a court. The changed text removes this specific allowance, implying that only monetary damages are available. The Law Explanation states that Section 38 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 allows for preventive relief by way of injunction. The removal of the court intervention language contradicts the intent of Section 38, as it suggests the parties have opted out of injunctive relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not helpful, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not helpful.
